args_redacted.query: zero trust security model principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy explained

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
        ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
-------------------
Zero Trust is a security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always
verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside the network
perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by
default — even if they are already inside the network. Every access request must
be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated before granting access.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
----------------
Defense in Depth (DiD) is a layered security strategy inspired by military
defense tactics. It deploys multiple, overlapping layers of security controls
across the entire IT infrastructure. The idea is that if one layer fails, the
next layer continues to provide protection. It covers physical, technical, and
administrative controls working in concert.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Never trust, always verify — every request is treated as potentially hostile
  • Least-privilege access — users and systems get only the minimum permissions
    necessary to perform their function
  • Micro-segmentation — networks are divided into small, isolated zones;
    lateral movement is severely restricted
  • Continuous monitoring and validation — authentication and authorization are
    ongoing, not one-time events at login
  • Assume breach — operate as if the network is already compromised
  • Explicit verification — use multiple signals (identity, device health,
    location, data classification) before granting access

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered protection — deploy controls at the perimeter, network, host,
    application, and data layers
  • No single point of failure — redundancy ensures that the failure of one
    control does not expose the entire system
  • Diversity of controls — use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating)
  • Physical, technical, and administrative controls — security spans people,
    processes, and technology
  • Defense at every layer — network firewalls, IDS/IPS, endpoint protection,
    encryption, access controls, security awareness training, etc.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers compensate for each       |
|                             | verify everything explicitly     | other's weaknesses               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Perimeter Reliance          | Perimeter is irrelevant;         | Perimeter is one important       |
|                             | security travels with the asset  | layer among many                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Granular, identity-centric,      | Often broader; access granted    |
|                             | per-session, per-resource        | once inside a trusted zone       |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Architecture        | Micro-segmented; flat networks   | Segmented but often with trusted |
|                             | are eliminated                   | internal zones                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Trust Model                 | Zero implicit trust              | Some implicit trust within       |
|                             |                                  | defined boundaries               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity, data, and workloads    | Infrastructure and network       |
|                             |                                  | topology                         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via           | Slowed but not always prevented  |
|                             | micro-segmentation               |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Authentication              | Continuous and adaptive          | Typically at session start;      |
|                             |                                  | may be periodic                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Historical Context          | Emerged ~2010 (Forrester);       | Military concept dating back     |
|                             | gained prominence with cloud     | centuries; adapted to IT in      |
|                             | and remote work                  | the 1990s-2000s                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+


4. STRENGTHS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST STRENGTHS:
  • Strong protection against insider threats — no one is

{"content": "================================================================================
        ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
-------------------
Zero Trust is a security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always
verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside the network
perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by
default — even if they are already inside the network. Every access request must
be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated before granting access.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
----------------
Defense in Depth (DiD) is a layered security strategy inspired by military
defense tactics. It deploys multiple, overlapping layers of security controls
across the entire IT infrastructure. The idea is that if one layer fails, the
next layer continues to provide protection. It covers physical, technical, and
administrative controls working in concert.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Never trust, always verify — every request is treated as potentially hostile
  • Least-privilege access — users and systems get only the minimum permissions
    necessary to perform their function
  • Micro-segmentation — networks are divided into small, isolated zones;
    lateral movement is severely restricted
  • Continuous monitoring and validation — authentication and authorization are
    ongoing, not one-time events at login
  • Assume breach — operate as if the network is already compromised
  • Explicit verification — use multiple signals (identity, device health,
    location, data classification) before granting access

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered protection — deploy controls at the perimeter, network, host,
    application, and data layers
  • No single point of failure — redundancy ensures that the failure of one
    control does not expose the entire system
  • Diversity of controls — use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating)
  • Physical, technical, and administrative controls — security spans people,
    processes, and technology
  • Defense at every layer — network firewalls, IDS/IPS, endpoint protection,
    encryption, access controls, security awareness training, etc.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers compensate for each       |
|                             | verify everything explicitly     | other's weaknesses               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Perimeter Reliance          | Perimeter is irrelevant;         | Perimeter is one important       |
|                             | security travels with the asset  | layer among many                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Granular, identity-centric,      | Often broader; access granted    |
|                             | per-session, per-resource        | once inside a trusted zone       |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Architecture        | Micro-segmented; flat networks   | Segmented but often with trusted |
|                             | are eliminated                   | internal zones                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Trust Model                 | Zero implicit trust              | Some implicit trust within       |
|                             |                                  | defined boundaries               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity, data, and workloads    | Infrastructure and network       |
|                             |                                  | topology                         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via           | Slowed but not always prevented  |
|                             | micro-segmentation               |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Authentication              | Continuous and adaptive          | Typically at session start;      |
|                             |                                  | may be periodic                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Historical Context          | Emerged ~2010 (Forrester);       | Military concept dating back     |
|                             | gained prominence with cloud     | centuries; adapted to IT in      |
|                             | and remote work                  | the 1990s-2000s                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+


4. STRENGTHS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST STRENGTHS:
  • Strong protection against insider threats — no one is trusted by default
  • Effective in cloud-native and hybrid environments — security is not tied
    to a physical network perimeter
  • Reduces the blast radius of a breach — micro-segmentation contains
    compromises to a very small area
  • Aligns

    lateral movement is severely restricted
  • Continuous monitoring and validation — authentication and authorization are
    ongoing, not one-time events at login
  • Assume breach — operate as if the network is already compromised
  • Explicit verification — use multiple signals (identity, device health,
    location, data classification) before granting access

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered protection — deploy controls at the perimeter, network, host,
    application, and data layers
  • No single point of failure — redundancy ensures that the failure of one
    control does not expose the entire system
  • Diversity of controls — use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating)
  • Physical, technical, and administrative controls — security spans people,
    processes, and technology
  • Defense at every layer — network firewalls, IDS/IPS, endpoint protection,
    encryption, access controls, security awareness training, etc.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers compensate for each       |
|                             | verify everything explicitly     | other's weaknesses               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Perimeter Reliance          | Perimeter is irrelevant;         | Perimeter is one important       |
|                             | security travels with the asset  | layer among many                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Granular, identity-centric,      | Often broader; access granted    |
|                             | per-session, per-resource        | once inside a trusted zone       |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Architecture        | Micro-segmented; flat networks   | Segmented but often with trusted |
|                             | are eliminated                   | internal zones                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Trust Model                 | Zero implicit trust              | Some implicit trust within       |
|                             |                                  | defined boundaries               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity, data, and workloads    | Infrastructure and network       |
|                             |                                  | topology                         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via           | Slowed but not always prevented  |
|                             | micro-segmentation               |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Authentication              | Continuous and adaptive          | Typically at session start;      |
|                             |                                  | may be periodic                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Historical Context          | Emerged ~2010 (Forrester);       | Military concept dating back     |
|                             | gained prominence with cloud     | centuries; adapted to IT in      |
|                             | and remote work                  | the 1990s-2000s                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+


4. STRENGTHS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST STRENGTHS:
  • Strong protection against insider threats — no one is trusted by default
  • Effective in cloud-native and hybrid environments — security is not tied
    to a physical

{"content": "================================================================================
        ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
-------------------
Zero Trust is a security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always
verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside the network
perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by
default — even if they are already inside the network. Every access request must
be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated before granting access.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
----------------
Defense in Depth (DiD) is a layered security strategy inspired by military
defense tactics. It deploys multiple, overlapping layers of security controls
across the entire IT infrastructure. The idea is that if one layer fails, the
next layer continues to provide protection. It covers physical, technical, and
administrative controls working in concert.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Never trust, always verify — every request is treated as potentially hostile
  • Least-privilege access — users and systems get only the minimum permissions
    necessary to perform their function
  • Micro-segmentation — networks are divided into small, isolated zones;
    lateral movement is severely restricted
  • Continuous monitoring and validation — authentication and authorization are
    ongoing, not one-time events at login
  • Assume breach — operate as if the network is already compromised
  • Explicit verification — use multiple signals (identity, device health,
    location, data classification) before granting access

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered protection — deploy controls at the perimeter, network, host,
    application, and data layers
  • No single point of failure — redundancy ensures that the failure of one
    control does not expose the entire system
  • Diversity of controls — use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating)
  • Physical, technical, and administrative controls — security spans people,
    processes, and technology
  • Defense at every layer — network firewalls, IDS/IPS, endpoint protection,
    encryption, access controls, security awareness training, etc.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers compensate for each       |
|                             | verify everything explicitly     | other's weaknesses               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Perimeter Reliance          | Perimeter is irrelevant;         | Perimeter is one important       |
|                             | security travels with the asset  | layer among many                 |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Granular, identity-centric,      | Often broader; access granted    |
|                             | per-session, per-resource        | once inside a trusted zone       |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Network Architecture        | Micro-segmented; flat networks   | Segmented but often with trusted |
|                             | are eliminated                   | internal zones                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Trust Model                 | Zero implicit trust              | Some implicit trust within       |
|                             |                                  | defined boundaries               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Identity, data, and workloads    | Infrastructure and network       |
|                             |                                  | topology                         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via           | Slowed but not always prevented  |
|                             | micro-segmentation               |                                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Authentication              | Continuous and adaptive          | Typically at session start;      |
|                             |                                  | may be periodic                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Historical Context          | Emerged ~2010 (Forrester);       | Military concept dating back     |
|                             | gained prominence with cloud     | centuries; adapted to IT in      |
|                             | and remote work                  | the 1990s-2000s                  |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+


4. STRENGTHS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST STRENGTHS:
  • Strong protection against insider threats — no one is trusted by default
  • Effective in cloud-native and hybrid environments — security is not tied
    to a physical network perimeter
